shower.thought: We teach children that lying is wrong, then spend their teenage years mad that they're honest about what we actually look like.
The worst part about having a good memory isn't remembering the bad things — it's remembering exactly how you felt about people who've since changed.
You can't miss something you never had, which is probably why we're so good at wanting what we've already lost.
Nostalgia is just grief wearing a comfortable sweater.
The moment you become fluent in something, you stop noticing how hard it is — which is why experts can't teach beginners.
We call it "living in the moment" but we're actually just describing the absence of anxiety about something.
Time moves slower when you're paying attention and faster when you're not, which means the most vivid parts of your life are the ones that felt longest but probably weren't.
You spend decades learning that your parents are just people, then panic when you realize you're becoming them.
The people who claim they don't care what anyone thinks are usually thinking about it the most.
We're nostalgic for decades we couldn't wait to escape.
Your phone knows more about you than you know about yourself, and you paid it to do that.
The only real difference between a hobby and an obsession is whether someone else pays for it.
We say we hate small talk, but we also hate the silence that follows honest ones.
Most of our fears are just problems that haven't happened yet, which means we're really talented at suffering in advance.
Boredom might be the only honest emotion we experience, because everything else is performed for someone.
You only realize how much someone shaped you after they stop being around to shape you.
The reason we can't sleep isn't usually that our bed is uncomfortable — it's that our brain finally has nothing to do.
We treat our attention like it's infinite and our time like it's finite, when it's actually the reverse.
Shame is just the gap between who you are and who you thought you were, and it closes as soon as you stop pretending they should be the same person.
Everyone's version of "finally doing what makes them happy" looks a lot like "stopping what made everyone else happy."

motivational.thought: The person you're becoming is already asking for help. Listen to them. — Unknown
Regret for the things we did can be tempered by time; it is regret for the things we did not do that is inconsolable. — Sydney Smith
Your life is not a dress rehearsal. Stop practicing and perform. — Unknown
The obstacle is the way, but only if you stop treating it as something other than the path. — Unknown
Most people fail not because they aim too high and miss, but because they aim too low and hit. — Les Brown
You don't rise to the level of your goals; you fall to the level of your systems. — Unknown
The only permission you need is your own. — Unknown
Suffering is optional. Struggle, on the other hand, is mandatory for anyone who wants something real. — Unknown
What would you attempt if you knew you couldn't fail? Now ask yourself why that knowledge should matter. — Unknown
The best time to plant a tree was 20 years ago. The second best time is today. — Ancient Proverb
Your future self is watching you right now through memories you haven't made yet. — Unknown
Discipline is choosing between what you want now and what you want most. — Unknown
The world doesn't pay you for the hours you work; it pays you for the value you create. — Unknown
Comparison is the death of contentment and the birth of ambition. Choose wisely which one you need. — Unknown
You cannot hate yourself into a version you love. — Unknown
The cost of discipline is always less than the cost of regret. — Unknown
Small disciplines repeated consistently over time create remarkable results. — Unknown
Everything you want is on the other side of fear, but fear is also on the other side of everything you want. — Unknown
Your standards are your ceiling. Raise them. — Unknown
What you tolerate will multiply. What you celebrate will multiply. Choose carefully. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
My therapist asked me what my biggest fear is.
I said, "Abandonment." She said, "We'll have to continue this next week," then checked her watch.

I told my friend he was drinking too much.
He said, "I'm not, you're just not drinking enough."

My dentist said I need to floss more.
I said, "I'll try." He said, "That's what you said last time." I said, "Exactly—I'm consistent."

I went to a motivational speaker who kept saying, "You can do anything!"
I asked for a refund. He said, "See? You already did something."

My girlfriend asked if I loved her more than video games.
I said, "Of course." She said, "Then why are you still holding the controller?" I said, "In case you're lying."

I told my boss I was having imposter syndrome.
He said, "Don't worry, you're not an imposter." I said, "Really?" He said, "No, you're exactly as incompetent as you think."

My mom said she'd always be there for me.
She moved to Florida. I said, "You're literally not here." She said, "Emotionally, honey. Emotionally."

I asked my barber why he kept cutting my hair shorter than I asked.
He said, "Because you're bald underneath." I said, "Then charge me less." He said, "I do."

My friend said he was going to eat healthier.
Three days later he was back to normal. I said, "That didn't last long." He said, "Neither will I."

I told my kid lying is wrong.
He asked why politicians do it then. I said, "Because they're good at it." He said, "So you're saying I should practice?"

My ex said she missed me.
I said, "That's nice." She said, "No, like—she genuinely cannot hit me anymore, I moved states."

I asked my accountant if I was doing okay financially.
He said, "Define okay." I said, "Like, not terrible?" He said, "Then no."

My friend said he wanted to be remembered.
He's been dead for three weeks and I haven't called anyone yet. I think he's going to get his wish.

I went to a self-help seminar on being confident.
The instructor asked for volunteers. Nobody raised their hand. He said, "See? Even I wouldn't pick any of you."

My dad said he regretted not pursuing his dreams.
I said, "It's never too late." He said, "I'm seventy-three." I said, "Exactly—you have no time to be disappointed."

I told my therapist I think everyone hates me.
She said, "I'm sure that's not true." I said, "You check your phone every five minutes during our sessions." She said, "Fair point."

My friend asked if I was mad at him.
I said, "No." He said, "But you haven't talked to me in a month." I said, "I said I wasn't mad."

I asked my doctor how long I have to live.
He said, "Can't say for certain." I said, "Give me an estimate." He said, "Well, you're overdue now."

My partner said I never listen to her.
At least that's what I think she said—I wasn't really paying attention.

My friend said he had good news and bad news.
I said, "Give me the good news." He said, "I'm moving to Japan." I said, "What's the bad news?" He said, "That's the good news."
